Belshazzar defied God’s judgments. Most historians believe Cyrus then besieged Babylon. Security and sensuality are sad evidence of approaching ruin. Mirth is truly sinful when it profanes sacred things; and many songs used at modern feasts are no better than the praises heathens sing to their gods! See how God struck terror upon Belshazzar and his lords. God’s written word is enough to frighten even the proudest, boldest sinner. What we see of God, the part of His hand that writes in creation and in Scripture, should fill us with awe concerning what we do not see. If this is the finger of God, what is His arm when it is revealed? And who is He? The king’s guilty conscience told him he had no reason to expect good news from heaven. God can, in a moment, make even the stoutest sinner tremble; and there needs only for Him to let loose his own thoughts upon him; they will cause enough trouble. No bodily pain can equal the inward agony which sometimes seizes the sinner in the midst of mirth, carnal pleasures, and worldly pomp. Sometimes terrors drive a man to Christ for pardon and peace; but many cry out from fear of wrath, who are not humbled for their sins, and who seek relief through empty things. The ignorance and uncertainty concerning the Holy Scriptures, shown by many who call themselves wise, only tends to drive sinners to despair, as the ignorance of these wise men did.

Daniel was forgotten at court; he lived privately, and was then ninety years old. Many consult God’s servants on difficult questions or to explain hard subjects, but without asking about salvation or the path of duty. Daniel rejected the offer of reward. He spoke to Belshazzar as if he were a condemned criminal. We should despise all the gifts and rewards this world can give if we saw, as we may by faith, its end approaching; but let us do our duty in the world, and do it with all the real service we can.

Daniel reads Belshazzar’s doom. He had not taken warning from the judgments upon Nebuchadnezzar. And he insulted God. Sinners are pleased with gods that neither see, nor hear, nor know; but they will be judged by One to whom all things are open. Daniel reads the sentence written on the wall. All this may well be applied to the doom of every sinner. At death, a sinner’s days are numbered and finished; after death comes judgment, when he will be weighed and found wanting; and after judgment the sinner will be destroyed and given as prey to the devil and his angels. While these things were happening in the palace, it is considered that Cyrus’ army entered the city; and when Belshazzar was slain, a general submission followed. Soon every impenitent sinner will find the writing of God's word brought to pass upon him, whether he is weighed in the balance of the law as a self-righteous Pharisee or in that of the gospel as a painted hypocrite.
